title: How driving in traffic activates the fight-or-flight system
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:
You're not imagining it. Your body knows what's happening. 

 I used to think I was just a bad driver. Or maybe an impatient person. Or perhaps someone wh...
htmlContent:
You're not imagining it. Your body knows what's happening.

I used to think I was just a bad driver. Or maybe an impatient person. Or perhaps someone who just needed to "calm down" and "breathe through it." You know the advice. The kind of advice that makes you feel worse because clearly you're failing at something as basic as sitting in a car.

Here's the thing. I've spent years studying how the nervous system works, both in my own body and through the research of people like Stephen Porges and Bessel van der Kolk. And what I've come to understand is that driving in traffic isn't just annoying. It's a legitimate threat to your nervous system. Your fight-or-flight response isn't overreacting. It's doing exactly what it evolved to do.

Think about it. You're sitting in a metal box moving at high speeds surrounded by other metal boxes moving at high speeds, and you have almost no control over what happens. Someone cuts you off. A brake light flashes. A truck drifts into your lane. Your body doesn't know this is just rush hour. Your body knows you're in danger.

What Stephen Porges taught me about traffic and the vagus nerve

Stephen Porges is the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory. I'm not going to get too technical here, but the basic idea is that your nervous system has three main states: social engagement (feeling safe and connected), fight-or-flight (mobilized for danger), and shutdown (immobilized when things feel hopeless).

Driving in traffic, especially heavy traffic, constantly activates the fight-or-flight state. Your vagus nerve, which runs from your brainstem down through your chest and abdomen, is constantly scanning for safety cues. When you're in traffic, it's picking up on a lot of cues that say "not safe." The honking. The sudden movements. The proximity of other vehicles. The unpredictability.

Your body doesn't have a separate system for "this is just a minor inconvenience." It has one threat-detection system, and it treats everything as potentially life-threatening until proven otherwise. And traffic? Traffic is full of ambiguous signals. That car might stop. That driver might be distracted. That truck might not see you. Your nervous system can't relax because it can't predict what's coming next.

Wild, right? Your body is doing exactly what it should be doing, and you're sitting there thinking you're broken.

The freeze response and why you can't just "relax"

Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, talks a lot about how trauma gets trapped in the body. But even if you don't have a trauma history, your nervous system can get stuck in certain patterns. Driving in traffic is a perfect example of a situation where your body might cycle between fight, flight, and freeze.

Fight looks like road rage. The shouting, the gesturing, the aggressive driving. Flight looks like anxiety. The racing heart, the sweaty palms, the urge to get out of the car or take an exit even if it's the wrong one. Freeze looks like dissociation. You zone out. You miss your exit. You arrive somewhere and don't remember the drive.

I've done all three. I've screamed at drivers who couldn't hear me. I've taken exits that added thirty minutes to my commute just to escape the highway. I've arrived at my destination with no memory of the last twenty minutes of driving.

And here's what nobody tells you. The freeze response is particularly insidious because it looks like calm. You might think you're handling traffic well because you're not reacting. But dissociation is not regulation. It's your body's last-ditch effort to survive when fighting or fleeing isn't possible.

For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice is Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link). It helps explain why different parts of you might react differently to the same trigger.

Why your childhood shows up in the driver's seat

This is the part that surprised me. I started noticing that my reactions to traffic weren't just about traffic. They were about my history. About growing up in a home where unpredictability meant danger. Where I had to stay hypervigilant to survive. Where I learned that something bad could happen at any moment and I had to be ready.

Janina Fisher, who works with trauma and dissociation, talks about how our survival strategies get triggered by situations that remind our bodies of past threats. Driving in traffic can be one of those situations. The lack of control. The need to stay alert. The feeling that if you relax for one second, something terrible will happen.

For people with a history of complex trauma, especially from childhood, the nervous system is already on high alert. Traffic just turns the volume up. Your body remembers. It remembers what it felt like to be unsafe, to have no control, to have to watch for danger constantly.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). Bessel van der Kolk's work explains why your body holds onto these patterns even when your mind knows you're "just" driving.

What actually helps when you're stuck in traffic

I'm not going to tell you to just breathe. That's not helpful, and it's not honest. When your nervous system is in fight-or-flight, breathing exercises can feel like a demand, not a support. So let me offer some things that have actually worked for me and for people I've worked with.

First, name what's happening. Say it out loud. "My heart is racing. My hands are gripping the wheel. I feel trapped." This isn't about making it go away. It's about letting your body know that you see it. That you're paying attention. That you're not ignoring its signal.

Second, give your body something to do that signals safety. Press your feet into the floor of the car. Squeeze and release your hands on the wheel. Turn down the music or turn it up, whichever feels better. Your body needs movement to complete the stress response, even if that movement is just small and contained.

Third, change your relationship to time. Traffic makes you late, and lateness triggers shame and fear for a lot of us. But here's the thing. You can't control traffic. You can only control how you respond to it. If you're always late, maybe the problem isn't traffic. Maybe it's that you're not giving yourself enough buffer time. Or maybe you need to accept that some days you'll be late and that's not a moral failure.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker's work directly addresses the shame and inner critic that can get activated when things don't go as planned.

Fourth, and this is the hard one, notice what happens in your body when you can't get out. When you're stuck. When there's no escape route. This is where a lot of us carry our deepest wounds. The feeling of being trapped. The feeling that no one sees you. The feeling that you have to handle it alone.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). It helps explain why being trapped in a car with no control can feel so deeply familiar.

What your body is trying to tell you

I'm not going to wrap this up neatly. I don't have a five-step plan to make traffic enjoyable. Honestly, I don't think that's possible for most of us. But I do think there's something important in just understanding what's happening.

Your body isn't broken. Your nervous system isn't defective. You're not a bad driver or a weak person. You're a human being with a nervous system that evolved to keep you alive, and it's doing its job. Traffic is genuinely activating. It's not just you.

Stay with me here. The next time you're stuck in traffic and you feel that familiar surge of frustration or anxiety or numbness, try this. Instead of fighting it or judging it, just notice. Notice your hands on the wheel. Notice your breath. Notice the tension in your shoulders. You don't have to change anything. Just notice.

And if you can, give yourself some compassion. You're doing something that's genuinely hard for your nervous system. You're surviving it. That counts for something.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link)
  • The Body Keeps the Score (paid link)
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link)
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link)
